coredata.dc:title: Effective UV and blue light blocking filters with high visible transparency utilizing graphene quantum dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Abd El-kawy, F.S.
Hammad, S.
Talaat, H.
Ghali, M.
coredata.dc:description: In the current technological era, our eyes suffer serious diseases as a consequence of exposure to ultraviolet and blue radiation from light-emitting devices. Accordingly, the development of materials with effective absorption abilities for UV and blue light is in high demand. Here, we report the synthesis of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) from Moringa oleifera leaves utilizing the hydrothermal technique. Different amounts from the prepared GQDs solution were added to the Poly(vinyl) alcohol (PVA) to fabricate UV and blue light-blocking films with concentrations of (0, 3, 5, 7, 9, 10 %). These films were examined to explore their blocking potential. Interestingly, the data demonstrate that GQDs/PVA films can block up to 97.68 % and 96.21 % of harmful UV and blue light, respectively, with transmittance greater than 70 % in the visible regions (>600 nm). Further, the films were applied to WLED commercial lamps, and it was found that with increasing GQD concentrations, the blue region decreased with a blocking ratio of up to 91.38 %, and the LED light was changed from cool (6310 K) to warm (3300 K) white LEDs. These results prove that the sustainably fabricated GQDs films have strong down photoconversion ability and remarkable absorption in the UV and blue light regions.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Stability of the GQDs/PVA blocking films

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: F. S. Abd El-Kawy acknowledges the Mission Sector of the Higher Education Ministry for their support through a Ph.D. Scholarship. This work was supported by STDF project 38311 from the STDF agency in Egypt.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The XRD profile of the synthesized GQDs; (b) TEM micrograph with right inset for high-resolution TEM image and the left inset is the histogram for the size distribution of GQDs derived from Moringa oleifera leaves.
(a) PL spectra at different excitation wavelengths; (b) Photograph of GQDs under UV light; (c) UV–vis absorption spectrum, PL excitation, and emission spectra of GQDs.
FT-IR spectrum of the synthesized GQDs.
(a) XPS full survey spectra of GQDs and high-resolution XPS spectrum of (b) C 1s; (c) O 1s; (d) N 1s.
(a) Photographs of 3 % and 10 % GQDs/PVA films under daylight; (b)Transmittance spectra of PVA and fabricated GQDs/PVA films.
The FTIR spectra of (a) pure PVA; (b) GQDs/PVA films.

(a) Emission spectra; (b) CIE chromaticity diagram under excitation of 395 nm; (c) UV blocking ratio of PVA and different concentrations of GQDs/PVA films. (d) photograph of the emitted cyan light from the GQDs solution when no GQDs/PVA films (I); 7 % GQDs/PVA (II) and 10 % GQDs/PVA film (III) is inserted between the UV torch and the GQDs solution.
(a) Emission spectra of blue chip and different concentrations of GQDs/PVA films; (b) blue light blocking ratio of PVA and different concentrations GQDs/PVA films.

Electroluminescence spectra of blue light emitted by (a) commercial lamp only of CCT 6310 K; (b) commercial lamp and PVA film; (c–g) commercial lamp and different concentrations of GQDs/PVA films attached to the lamp; (h) CIE chromaticity coordinates of different GQDs/PVA films blocking blue light from WLEDs commercial lamp.

Electroluminescence spectra of blue light emitted by (a) commercial lamp only of CCT 12220 K; (b) commercial lamp and PVA film; (c–g) commercial lamp and different concentrations of GQDs/PVA films attached to the lamp; (h) CIE chromaticity coordinates of different GQDs/PVA films blocking blue light from WLEDs commercial lamp.

Electroluminescence spectra of blue light emitted by (a) commercial lamp only of CCT 3150 K; (b) commercial lamp and PVA film; (c–g) commercial lamp and different concentrations of GQDs/PVA films attached to the lamp; (h) CIE chromaticity coordinates of different GQDs/PVA films blocking blue light from WLEDs commercial lamp.
UV–vis light transmittance spectra of GQDs/PVA (a) before and after 15 days of UV irradiation; (b) before and after 2hrs of heating at 100 °C.
The Electroluminescence spectra of WLEDs with CCT of (a–c) 6310 K; (d–f) 12220 K; (g–i) 3150 K attached to different concentrations of GQDs/PVA films (5,7,10 %) for 24 h working time.
Comparison between recent studies on carbon dots-based materials used for UV and blue light shielding and that using our Moringa leaves Graphene quantum dots.

head.ce:title: Effective UV and blue light blocking filters with high visible transparency utilizing graphene quantum dots

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Basic and Applied Science
Egypt-Japan University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the current technological era, our eyes suffer serious diseases as a consequence of exposure to ultraviolet and blue radiation from light-emitting devices. Accordingly, the development of materials with effective absorption abilities for UV and blue light is in high demand. Here, we report the synthesis of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) from Moringa oleifera leaves utilizing the hydrothermal technique. Different amounts from the prepared GQDs solution were added to the Poly(vinyl) alcohol (PVA) to fabricate UV and blue light-blocking films with concentrations of (0, 3, 5, 7, 9, 10 %). These films were examined to explore their blocking potential. Interestingly, the data demonstrate that GQDs/PVA films can block up to 97.68 % and 96.21 % of harmful UV and blue light, respectively, with transmittance greater than 70 % in the visible regions (>600 nm). Further, the films were applied to WLED commercial lamps, and it was found that with increasing GQD concentrations, the blue region decreased with a blocking ratio of up to 91.38 %, and the LED light was changed from cool (6310 K) to warm (3300 K) white LEDs. These results prove that the sustainably fabricated GQDs films have strong down photoconversion ability and remarkable absorption in the UV and blue light regions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Light emitting diodes (LEDs) are rapidly coming into widespread use for applications such as general illumination, backlighting, and traffic lights owing to their low generation of heat, long lifetime, and high energy efficiency [1–5]. Typical White LEDs (WLED) emit intense blue or UV light in the wavelength between 380 and 500 nm, and the exposure to their radiation for long time leads to a reduction in retinal pigmented epithelium melanin and cause eye strain as well as macular degeneration [6–11]. UV and blue light cut-off filters and attenuating intraocular lenses have been used to overcome these problems; however, most of the filters are not able to effectively block both blue and UV light and reflect undesirable wavelengths instead of transform them into longer wavelengths to minimize light loss [12]. Numerous researches have been conducted in this regard using luminescent materials such as rare earths, semiconductor quantum dots, and carbon dots due to their light absorption and conversion properties [13–17]. For instance, Gong et al., synthesized carbon nanodots (CDs) using urea and citric acid [18]. They used carboxymethyl cellulose matrix in preparing CDs films (CDs@cel) and found that the films could achieve high shielding of UV about 99 %, and high energy blue light (HEBL) 99 %. Li et al., obtained two types of CdS QDs with cubic lattice and single hexagonal by changing the temperature of the solvothermal technique [19]. They fabricated nanocomposite films by dispersing CdS QDs in N,N-dimethacrylamide (DMA). The nanocomposite films exhibit high absorption spectra of UV and blue light under 440 nm. Nevertheless; the toxicity of semiconductor quantum dots limits their use.

Graphene quantum dots represent zero-dimensional graphene materials with a lateral size less than 100 nm [20]. There are several approaches to synthesize GQDs using different precursors [21–27]. However, the utilization of green, sustainable raw materials and cost-effective methods is still the idealistic route in producing GQDs. GQDs exhibit intriguing optical, thermal, and electrical properties [28]. Moreover, they possess good transparency to visible light and have strong absorption peaks in the UV region that make them promising candidates for UV filters [29,30]. GQDs were introduced to polymer systems in order to increase their transparency and stability [31]. Poly(vinyl) alcohol (PVA) is a non-toxic, low-cost and biodegradable polymer that facilitates the formation of stable and transparent GQDs films with higher UV-radiation protection comparable to other polymers [32]. Moringa leaves are chosen as raw materials for the synthesis of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as they are rich in organic carbon, providing an abundant source of carbon necessary for GQD synthesis [33]. Furthermore, Moringa leaves contain ample of phytochemical compounds such as polyphenols, alkaloids, and terpenoids that contain oxygenated functional groups (–OH, –COOH, –CO), which help introduce surface defects in GQDs. These defects create trap states that influence the recombination of excited electrons and holes, enhancing or tuning PL properties [34]. Moreover, polyphenols have π-conjugated systems that can interact with the graphene-like structure of GQDs and enhance the π–π stacking effect, which improves electron mobility and modifies PL emission properties. Additionally, Moringa is a fast-growing tree that produces a large number of leaves year-round, making it a more sustainable and easily available source compared to other slow-growing plants. In this work, green graphene quantum dots were fabricated from Moringa oleifera leaves and then dispersed with different proportions in PVA to obtain various blocking films. These films were applied to UV, blue chip and WLEDs commercial lamps to investigate their ability in blocking the harmful irradiation.

Moringa oleifera leaves were collected and washed several times, then dried and ground to fine powder. The dried leaf powder (1 g) was further added to deionized water (50 mL) and then sonicated using prob-sonicator for 15 min. Afterward, the solution was transferred into a stainless-steel autoclave with a Teflon liner of 70 mL capacity and placed in an oven for 8 h at 200 °C. The obtained solution after the reaction was left to cool down before filtration with Whatman filter paper and 0.22 μm syringe to eliminate the large aggregates. A dialysis membrane of molecular weight cutoff 1000 Da was used for 48 h to purify the filtered solution from the unreacted particles. Finally, the GQDs powder was obtained by freeze drying the GQDs solution for 72 h.
2
Fabrication of GQDs/PVA optical blocking films

Poly(vinyl) alcohol (2 g) was dispersed in deionized water (40 mL) and vigorously stirred at 95 °C until it becomes transparent and subsequently cooled to room temperature. The PVA solution was then transferred into six beakers, and appropriate proportions of GQDs solution (5 mg/mL) were added to the beakers to obtain six different concentrations of GQDs into PVA namely (0, 3, 5, 7, 9, 10 %). Each of these concentrations were stirred for 30 min and drop casted into glass substrates then allowed to dry at 60 °C for one day. Afterward, the GQDs/PVA films were applied to commercial lamp, blue and UV chip to monitor the blocked blue and UV light emission spectrum and the chromaticity coordinates.
3
Characterization of GQDs and GQDs/PVA optical blocking films

The morphological structure and size of the prepared GQDs were examined by transmission electron microscope (TEM) model of (JEOL JEM 2100-F) operating under acceleration voltage of 200 kV. X-ray diffraction data was acquired with Shimadzu XRD-6100 instrument, with a wavelength of 1.546 Å. FT-IR spectrum was obtained using interferometer of Bruker Invenio S and X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were measured with a K-ALPHA instrument (Themo Fisher Scientific, USA). The UV–Visible spectra of GQDs solution and transmittance spectra of the films were measured using Hitachi-U3900 spectrophotometer. The absolute quantum yield was measured directly using SC-30 integrating sphere of FS5 spectrofluorometer. The module of integrating sphere comprises of a hollow sphere of 150 mm diameter with the inner surface machined from a PTFE-based material for optimum reflectance. The optical blocking films were investigated by LMS-9000 Lisun CCD spectroradiometer.

The XRD pattern of the synthesized GQDs is depicted in Fig. 1 a and reveal a diffraction peak at 2θ = 23.8° that can be assigned to the crystallographic (002) plane of GQDs. The presence of the typical (002) plane signifies that the synthesized GQDs possess graphitic defects with functionalized oxygen groups. Fig. 1b displays a TEM image of the synthesized GQDs which appear as tiny sheets with hexagonal shape and average size 7 nm are clearly seen (the left inset of Fig. 1b). The interplanar distance was found to be 0.21 nm that can be assigned to the (100) plane of graphitic structure (inset of Fig. 1a).

The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the GQDs solution from 320 to 500 nm in 10 nm increments are displayed in Fig. 2 a. The spectra exhibit excitation-dependent emission, and the maximum emission is obtained at 340 nm excitation wavelength with a Stokes shift between photoluminescence excitation and emission of approximately 80 nm of approximately 80 nm. Additionally, there is a red shift in the emitted peaks with increasing the excitation wavelength which can be assigned to the surface states transitions related to oxygen function group [35]. As can be seen in Fig. 2b, the sample displays intense cyan fluorescence upon excitation with 400 nm UV light. The UV–vis absorption spectrum reveals two absorption peaks; one is located at 245 nm, which originates from the π-π∗ transition of the CC aromatic ring of the SP2 domain, and the other one is related to n-π∗ transitions of the CO bonds at 310 nm, as shown in Fig. 2c. The absolute quantum yield of the obtained GQDs was found to be 8.65 % at an excitation wavelength of 340 nm. This relatively small value of the quantum yield may be attributed to the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups which act as emissive traps leading to a reduction in the quantum yield [36]. However, this quantum yield value is still higher than that of the synthesized carbon-based dots prepared from other plants as precursors [37–39].

The investigation of the surface compositions functional groups has been done using Fourier transform IR (FT-IR) spectroscopy. Fig. 3 illustrates that the synthesized GQDs exhibit an absorption band at 3404 cm−1, attributed to the stretching vibration of the –NH/–OH bond. There are characteristic peaks at 1590 and 1402 cm−1 ascribed to CC, aromatic SP2. The faint peak at 1450 cm−1 corresponds to the C–N bond, indicating the presence of amine groups during the hydrothermal process. The absorption band at 1650 cm−1 is attributed to CO. The detected peaks at 1084 and 2967 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of C–O and C–H bonds, respectively. These various functional groups resulting in the synthesized GQDs are due to the phytochemicals exist in Moringa oleifera leaves such as steroids, terpenes, alkaloids, saponins, flavonoids and phenolic acid that contain carbon, oxygen and nitrogen active functional groups.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) is of paramount importance for identifying the elemental and chemical compositions of the as-prepared GQDs. The full-survey spectrum in Fig. 4 a depicts a prominent C 1s peak at 285.35 eV, O 1s peak located at 532.17 eV, and N 1s peak at 400.16 eV. The atomic percentages of the three elements are 71.3, 21.88, and 6.83 %, respectively. The deconvolution of the high-resolution spectrum of C1s in Fig. 4b reveals three peaks at 284.27 eV, 285.7 eV, and 287.52 eV, which are ascribed to CC, C–C/C–NH2, and the carbonyl carbons (CO), respectively. Fig. 4c illustrates that the O 1s spectrum consists of two carbon-oxygen bonds, namely CO and C–O, located at 532.14 eV and 530.9 eV. The two peaks in the N 1s spectrum, located at 399.28 eV and 400.96 eV, respectively, correspond to amino and quaternary N (Fig. 4d). The abovementioned results are in accordance with FTIR findings regarding the existence of multiple oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups that arise from the carbonization of phytochemicals in Moringa leaves.

The GQDs/PVA films were prepared by adding different amount of GQDs solution to the PVA solution to get six different concentrations of the GQDs namely 0, 3, 5, 7, 9, 10 %. The different concentrations were then drop cast on glass substrates to obtain the required films. The color of the films under daylight changes from colorless to light brown depending on the concentration of the dots without affecting the visibility of the object through the films. Fig. 5 a shows photographs of 3 % and 10 % GQDs/PVA films. The transmittance spectra of PVA and GQDs/PVA films are illustrated in Fig. 5b. The PVA film has high transmittance of about 90 %. As long as the concentration of GQDs in the PVA increases, the transmittance at UV to blue spectrum (380–500 nm) decreases indicating that the films exhibit significant light absorption in these regions.

As has been mentioned before, GQDs have abundant oxygen-containing functional groups (e.g., hydroxyl, carbonyl), allowing strong hydrogen bonding with PVA chains. This ensures excellent dispersion in the polymer matrix, preventing aggregation and enhancing multiple scattering events that improve light trapping and absorption in the GQDs/PVA thin films. The latter facilitates efficient UV-to-visible down-conversion. The formation of hydrogen bonding in the GQDs/PVA film can be confirmed from FTIR characterization of PVA and GQDs/PVA films. As shown in Fig. 6 (a and b) FTIR spectra of the PVA and GQDs/PVA reveal that the OH stretching at 3282 cm−1 and OH bending at 1085 cm−1 of PVA changed to a lower wavenumber of 3268 cm−1 and 1076 cm−1 respectively confirming the formation of hydrogen bonds between the PVA molecule and polar materials, which is consistent with the literature [40–42]. Additionally, the GQDs/PVA composite's FTIR spectra showed a change in the intensity of OH groups, which further suggests that a hydrogen bond has formed between the PVA molecule and the polar surface of GQDs [43].”

The GQDs/PVA films were examined to assess their ability as UV and blue light blocking films using UV LED (395 ± 5 nm) and blue LED (460 ± 5 nm). For the UV LED, as shown in Fig. 7 a, the fabricated films exhibit a broad emission spectrum from green to red when they are exposed to UV light. Additionally, the films absorb more photons and convert them into longer wavelengths with the increase of GQDs concentrations, which is consistent with the International Commission on Illumination (CIE) coordinates in Fig. 7b. These findings mean that our GQDs/PVA films can not only be used as blocking films but also as photoconverters to minimize light loss when preventing undesirable UV radiations.

The blocking ratios can be expressed as how effectively a material blocks UV/blue radiation compared to a reference. It quantifies how much less UV/blue light passes through or is emitted from a sample compared to a reference. The blocking ratios using the EL spectrum for the UV and blue excitation sources were calculated using the following equation
Blocking
ratios
( % )
=
(
1 −
I
sample , λ
I
ref , λ
)
∗ 100
Where I sample, λ is the EL intensity of the GQDs/PVA film at the wavelength of the excitation source (λ) (UV = 395 nm, blue = 460 nm). I ref, λ is the EL intensity of the reference (without the GQDs/PVA film) at the wavelength of the excitation source (λ) (UV = 395 nm, blue = 460 nm). The UV blocking ratios of GQDs/PVA films are illustrated in Fig. 7c. It is clear that the PVA film is not efficient as it blocks only 16.23 % from UV-radiation; nevertheless, the GQDs/PVA films with GQDs concentrations of 3, 5, 7, 9, 10 % can block 46.87, 62.75, 87.76, 95.46, 97.68 %, respectively which is very high percentages and confirms that our green GQDs are efficient UV cut-off filters. Another simple experiment was conducted to examine the effectiveness of our GQDs/PVA films in blocking UV-radiation. When a UV torch is placed on top of as-synthesized GQDs solution directly, we noticed a strong cyan luminescence from the GQDs solution (Fig. 7d: I). Nevertheless, a weak emission from the GQDs solution results when placing our GQDs/PVA film, with concentration of 7 % between the UV torch and GQDs solution (Fig. 7d: II). Furthermore, when we replace the 7 % GQDs/PVA film with 10 % GQDs/PVA film, most of the incident UV is absorbed and no luminescence from the GQDs solution I noticed by naked eye Fig. 7d: III). These findings are consistent with the obtained blocking ratios of our GQDs/PVA films.

In order to test the efficiency of our GQDs/PVA film as a blue light cut-off filter, we conducted another experiment, namely, the as-synthesized GQDs/PVA films were placed on the top of blue LED chip and the intensity of the transmitted blue light was measured. As shown in Fig. 8 a, the intensity of the transmitted blue when using only PVA film, was found to be very high, then the intensity of the transmitted blue light starts to decline upon using GQDs/PVA film with different GQDs concentrations until it reaches its minimum intensity at GQDs concentration of 10 %. Fig. 8b shows the change in the percentage of the blue light blocking ratio with the GQDs concentration in the GQDs/PVA films. As seen, there is an increment in the blocking ratio, at 460 nm, with increasing the GQDs concentration. Namely, the blocking ratio of the GQDs/PVA films is 20.23 %, 47.87 %, 79.57 %, 84.82 %, 94.55 %, and 96.21 % for 0, 3, 5, 7, 9, and 10 % GQDs/PVA films, respectively. The high values of the blue blocking ratio make our films excellent candidates for blue light filters.

In another experiment, our GQDs/PVA films were examined in practical application using White LEDs commercial lamp with color correlated temperature of 6310 K. It is found that, the higher the concentration of GQDs in the GQDs/PVA films, the more the blue light emitted from the commercial lamp is blocked. The PVA film transmits most of the blue light, while the GQDs/PVA films block enormous proportions of the emitted blue light accompanied with an increase of green and red light intensities (Fig. 9 a–g). As shown in Fig. 9h, the CIE coordinates of WLED commercial lamp transformed from white (0.31, 0.33) to orange (0.43, 0.44) after placing the GQDs/PVA film on the top of the WLED lamp. This finding is a further confirmation of the ability of our GQDs/PVA films to absorb short wavelength and convert them into longer wavelength emission. Moreover, the color correlated temperature of the WLED lamp also changed from 6310 K (cool) to 3300 K (warm white LEDs), which is favorable for rendering the true colors of the objects.

A variety of LED lamps with different color temperatures were used to examine the films performance (to ensure that our results can be applied in the real world). Therefore, we investigated the ability of the fabricated GQDs/PVA films to block the blue light emitted by LED lamps with color temperatures of 12220 and 3150 K. Figs. 10 and 11
present the electroluminescence spectra emitted by commercial WLEDs with CCT of 12220 and 3150 K, respectively. It is found that the blue light intensity of WLEDs with only PVA film is nearly indistinguishable from that without the film, as illustrated in the new Figs. 10 and 11 (a, b). For the GQDs/PVA films, the blue region declined in comparison to the yellow region in the spectra as GQDs percentage increased (Figs. 10 and 11 (c–g)). Consequently, as illustrated in Fig. 10h the correlated color temperature (CCT) value of the light source with a CCT of 12220 was decreased to 3038 K. In the case of using another light source with a CCT of 3150K, the CCT was decreased to 2202 K as shown in Fig. 11h. Moreover, it was found that the CIE coordinates were changed from (0.2746, 0.2713) to (0.4444, 0.4245) and from (0.4270, 0.3997) to (0.5231, 0.4408) for light source with CCT of 12220 and 3150 K, respectively. These results demonstrate that despite the multiple light sources, the fabricated GQDs/PVA films are capable of preventing the blue light effectively and can be applied to various light sources in our daily life.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability of the GQDs/PVA blocking films
ce:section.ce:para: The stability of the GQDs/PVA film in UV and blue light shielding performance is essential for long-term applications. Therefore, the GQDs/PVA film (10 %) was subjected to a UV light source (λ = 400 nm) for 15 days and subsequently the transmittance spectra before and after the duration of continuous irradiation was measured. Fig. 12 a demonstrates that unsignificant change is found in the transmittance spectra with the UV exposure. As for the thermal stability of the GQDs/PVA film, the GQDs/PVA (10 %) film was heated for 2h at 100 °C and the transmittance spectrum was recorded. It was found that the film exhibited no significant change in the transmission spectra (Fig. 12b). from these results we can conclude that the GQDs/PVA films are able to maintain their capability to absorb the UV and blue light even when exposed to higher temperatures for long time.

To further assess the optical stability of the fabricated GQDs/PVA films when exposed to long-term light, additional experiments using White LEDs commercial lamps with CCT of 12220, 6310, and 3150 K was conducted. Namely, Different concentrations of GQDs/PVA films (5, 7, and 10 %) were selected for testing by applying them to the commercial White LEDs lamps for long durations. Fig. 13 a-i presents the EL spectra of the lamps (with the GQDs/PVA films) after continuous operation for 24 h. As shown, there is no change in the spectra during the whole duration. These results prove that our GQDs/PVA film's UV and blue light blocking behavior remained unchanged with long-term light.

Table 1 summarizes the shielding properties and the transparency of carbon dots-based film from literature and their potential in UV and blue light blocking processes compared with our synthesized GQDs. As shown, most of the prior researches cannot achieve sizable UV and blue light protection at the same time with high transparency films from green and cost-effective raw materials. For instance, Park et al., used acetone to obtain carbon dots which were then utilized in fabricating UV and blue light blocking films with bocking ratio of 95.9 and 82.3 %, respectively [8]. However, breathing or swallowing acetone can lead to nausea, racing pulse and eye irritation. Also, highly efficient UV and blue light shielding films were produced by chemically synthesized carbon dots using urea and citric acid [18]. Although a green method was utilized to obtain UV blocking films from graphene sheets, derived from sugar beet leaves, the blocking ratio of the harmful UV radiations did not exceed 70 % with low film transparency of only 60 % [28]. Citric acid and ethylenediamine were used as precursors for nitrogen doped carbon dots (NCDs) that were added to hydroxypropyl methylcellulose to fabricate NCDs@cel films [44]. The results revealed that the fabricated films have an efficient UV blocking ratio of 95 % and only 50 % for blue light blocking along with 60 % of visible transparency. Our green synthesized GQDs were prepared from Moringa oleifera leaves and water only, where we obtained GQDs films which exhibit high UV blocking ratio (97 %) as well as high blue light blocking up to 96.21 % and maintain good visible transparency of 70 %. These findings confirm that our sustainable and eco-friendly synthesized GQDs/PVA films have efficient UV and blue light absorption, and are more efficient than commercial UV and blue light filters produced from carbon dots prepared from other sources.

A sustainable method was adopted for synthesis of graphene quantum dots from Moringa oleifera leaves and deionized water. The as-synthesized GQDs are incorporated into PVA in different amounts to obtain various concentrations of GQDs/PVA films. These films exhibit a high degree of light blocking in the UV and blue bands that can reach 97.68 % and 96.21 %, respectively. Moreover, the fabricated films can convert the absorbed short wavelengths to longer wavelengths so the light loss can be minimized while preventing undesirable radiation. It is demonstrated that GQDs/PVA films can transform cool WLED lamps with CIE coordinates of (0.31, 0.33) and CCT of 6310 K into warm WLEDs with (0.43, 0.44) and CCT of 3300 K. Therefore, our films can be considered as cost-effective optical barriers for reducing eye damage caused by general lighting and harmful rays.

F.S. Abd El-kawy: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. S. Hammad: Writing – review & editing. H. Talaat: Writing – review & editing. M. Ghali: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Investigation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: F. S. Abd El-Kawy acknowledges the Mission Sector of the Higher Education Ministry for their support through a Ph.D. Scholarship. This work was supported by STDF project 38311 from the STDF agency in Egypt.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Rational design of multicolor-emitting chiral carbonized polymer dots for full-color and white circularly polarized luminescence

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ru, Y., Sui, L., Song, H., Liu, X., Tang, Z., Zang, S.-Q., Yang, B., & Lu, S.
